Very interesting. What are some other mistakes people make when it comes to AI and using it for either creativity, for headlines, for bullets, for anything? Yeah, one thing to keep in mind is that it's a language model. So chatGPT and GPT-3 are language models, which means that you can do research up to a point, but don't expect it, don't expect the facts that it gives you back to be accurate every single time. Because it's not trained to be a scientific researcher, for example. So it is more prone to give you language, scientific language, than it is to give you scientific fact. Now, there are other AI tools like Consensus and Enlisted and so on that are specific models trained on scientific papers. Those AIs, so to speak, are better to use if you're looking for factual, accurate, mathematical, scientific, very accurate language, not just language, but like facts. And then what you do is you take the facts from Consensus, you do a Consensus or Enlisted, you take that language and you put it into chatGPT and you ask it to rewrite. So if a paper, let's say a scientific paper has a blurb or a section on the benefits of banana on pizza, and it talks about the chemicals that happen when it bakes and the good things in banana that's good for your body and your brain and your health and the good things in pizza that's good for your body and health, you take that chunk of text, these are facts, you take that text and you put it into chatGPT and you ask it to rewrite it into 20 bullets. You ask it to rewrite it into the paySOP formula to express like if you don't eat your bananas every day, then these things will happen to your body and then you'll be dead within a year or something crazy. And then so you take scientific facts and then you put it into the language model. So understanding that there are thousand different types of AI or machine learning tools, each have a unique purpose and use and don't try to use it for what is not built for, if that makes sense. So a couple of, so I already said it needs to be specific and you need to be descriptive in your language. So you need to give it a clear function to do like write, rewrite, analyze, you need to be clear about what you're looking for. Not just give me 10 headlines, write or rewrite or analyze. So you got to be specific in function and then you and the mistake people often make also is that they don't give it enough context. So they don't give it enough information about let's say the avatar or the product or the things that the avatar is hoping for or want or desire or fear. So you got to give it context, enough context that if I was to describe it to a human, they would understand like how that be your litmus test is that if I explain something to you, David, cold, here's my avatar, here's the product. If I then just, if I'm one sentence on each and I say, David, write me 10 headlines, you'd be like, what? I can't write you 10. I don't know enough to write you 10 headlines or 10 bullets. Like there's not enough information for me to do it. Same thing with AI, so to speak, give it enough context so to understand. So I use human words, understand, even though it doesn't understand, but I'll use that anyway. So it understands the context because it'll give you far better output. And then use modifiers. These are words or phrases that adjust or refine the output. This is why I said earlier, you need to become best friends with adjectives and synonyms and modifiers like length, tone, style, what to focus on, what to leave out. Like if I'm looking for bullets that express only negative emotions, I will even say don't, do not include solutions or positive sentiment in these bullets. So just being specific and descriptive in the kind of output that you're looking for and the kind of output that you want, that'll go a long, long way for you to get good quality output. And also, like finally, don't ask it for opinions or to make a judgment call or facts. It'll, at this point, it'll decline and say, I can't do that. I'm a language model, blah, blah, blah. Instead, what you want to do is to give it and assign it a role and have it pretend to be something. To the point where I will say, if I'm doing, as a financial research and try to use it for it to get a sense of just generally what do investors think and feel and desire, I will tell it, you are a financial and investing expert. You know, everything there is to know about stock investing, options to investing and how to make money from the stock market. You have years of experience and a PhD level understanding of how investing works. I've given it a function and a role. Then I will say, based on what you know, and looking back over the past 20 years, what are the best, the five best trades that an investor could have made in any stock and with any investing strategy. Then it'll give me those examples. But if I just said, what are the five best investments of the past 10 years, it'll probably say, I don't know. I'm a language model. I can't give you that. But if you, it's like role playing and acting. If you ask it to assume a role that's specific to what you're looking for, your output will become much better, much stronger, and you'll also get useful output as opposed to, sorry, can't do it. Interesting.
